Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>

According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[Japanese literature]] and ''[[Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Dogū]] * [[Familiar]] * [[Haniwa]] * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Kokeshi]] * [[Paper doll]] * [[Pelesit]] * [[Poppet]] * [[Spirit animal (disambiguation)]] * [[Totem]] * [[Ushabti]] * [[Voodoo doll]] * [[Zuijin]]
==References== {{reflist}}

{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[Japan]]ese [[folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[Japanese literature]] and ''[[Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>

Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>

According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[Japanese literature]] and ''[[Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Dogū]] * [[Familiar]] * [[Haniwa]] * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Kokeshi]] * [[Paper doll]] * [[Pelesit]] * [[Poppet]] * [[Spirit animal (disambiguation)]] * [[Totem]] * [[Ushabti]] * [[Voodoo doll]] * [[Zuijin]]
==References== {{reflist}}

Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Term for a being from Japanese folklore}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:category:kk]][[category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{distinct|Shinigami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=式神|date=March 2020}} [[File:Fudo-Rieki-Engi 01.jpg|right|thumb|240px|[[Abe no Seimei]] and his {{transl|ja|shikigami}} (bottom right) before an assembly of god-like demon spirits]]
{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[Japan]]ese [[folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[Japanese literature]] and ''[[Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Dogū]] * [[Familiar]] * [[Haniwa]] * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Kokeshi]] * [[Paper doll]] * [[Pelesit]] * [[Poppet]] * [[Spirit animal (disambiguation)]] * [[Totem]] * [[Ushabti]] * [[Voodoo doll]] * [[Zuijin]]
==References== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Term for a being from Japanese folklore}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{distinct|Shinigami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=式神|date=March 2020}} [[File:Fudo-Rieki-Engi 01.jpg|right|thumb|240px|[[Abe no Seimei]] and his {{transl|ja|shikigami}} (bottom right) before an assembly of god-like demon spirits]]
{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[Japan]]ese [[folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[Japanese literature]] and ''[[Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Dogū]] * [[Familiar]] * [[Haniwa]] * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Kokeshi]] * [[Paper doll]] * [[Pelesit]] * [[Poppet]] * [[Spirit animal (disambiguation)]] * [[Totem]] * [[Ushabti]] * [[Voodoo doll]] * [[Zuijin]]
==References== {{reflist}}
{{Japanese folklore long}}
[[Category:Japanese folklore]] [[Category:Japanese ghosts]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Onmyōdō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Shikigami]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Shikigami]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Shikigami]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Shikigami]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Shikigami]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Shikigami]]

There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>

Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>

{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[Japan]]ese [[folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[Japanese literature]] and ''[[Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>

It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[Japanese literature]] and ''[[Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Dogū]] * [[Familiar]] * [[Haniwa]] * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Kokeshi]] * [[Paper doll]] * [[Pelesit]] * [[Poppet]] * [[Spirit animal (disambiguation)]] * [[Totem]] * [[Ushabti]] * [[Voodoo doll]] * [[Zuijin]]

''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Term for a being from Japanese folklore}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{distinct|Shinigami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=式神|date=March 2020}} [[File:Fudo-Rieki-Engi 01.jpg|right|thumb|240px|[[Abe no Seimei]] and his {{transl|ja|shikigami}} (bottom right) before an assembly of god-like demon spirits]]
{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[Japan]]ese [[folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[Japanese literature]] and ''[[Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Dogū]] * [[Familiar]] * [[Haniwa]] * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Kokeshi]] * [[Paper doll]] * [[Pelesit]] * [[Poppet]] * [[Spirit animal (disambiguation)]] * [[Totem]] * [[Ushabti]] * [[Voodoo doll]] * [[Zuijin]]
==References== {{reflist}}
{{Japanese folklore long}}

The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>

{{Short description|Term for a being from Japanese folklore}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{distinct|Shinigami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=式神|date=March 2020}} [[File:Fudo-Rieki-Engi 01.jpg|right|thumb|240px|[[Abe no Seimei]] and his {{transl|ja|shikigami}} (bottom right) before an assembly of god-like demon spirits]]
{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[Japan]]ese [[folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[Japanese literature]] and ''[[Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[Dogū]] * [[Familiar]] * [[Haniwa]] * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[Kokeshi]] * [[Paper doll]] * [[Pelesit]] * [[Poppet]] * [[Spirit animal (disambiguation)]] * [[Totem]] * [[Ushabti]] * [[Voodoo doll]] * [[Zuijin]]
==References== {{reflist}}

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1573015]] Wikidata name: shikigami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Term for a being from Japanese folklore}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{distinct|Shinigami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=式神|date=March 2020}} [[File:Fudo-Rieki-Engi 01.jpg|right|thumb|240px|[[Abe no Seimei]] and his {{transl|ja|shikigami}} (bottom right) before an assembly of god-like demon spirits]]
{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese [[:en:Folklore|Folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[:en:Inoue Nobutaka|Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[:en:Ghost|Ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] and ''[[:en:Emakimono|Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[:en:Folk religion|Folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[:en:Oni|Oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[:en:Megumi Fushiguro|Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[:en:Dogū|Dogū]] * [[:en:Familiar|Familiar]] * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[:en:Kokeshi|Kokeshi]] * [[:en:Paper doll|Paper doll]] * [[:en:Pelesit|Pelesit]] * [[:en:Poppet|Poppet]] * [[:en:Spirit animal (disambiguation)|Spirit animal (disambiguation)]] * [[:en:Totem|Totem]] * [[:en:Ushabti|Ushabti]] * [[:en:Voodoo doll|Voodoo doll]] * [[:en:Zuijin|Zuijin]]
==References== {{reflist}}
{{Japanese folklore long}} ]]

}}

revision.comment: [[階級]]へのリンクを解消、リンクを除去([[User:ネイ/DisamAssist|DisamAssist]]使用)
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2011年12月}} {{multiple image | align = right | direction = vertical | header = | header_align = center | footer = | image1 = Fudo-Rieki-Engi 01.jpg | width1 = 270 | caption1 = 『不動利益縁起絵巻』（部分）
本作は[[園城寺]]（三井寺）の[[縁起]]の一つである『不動利益縁起』を著した[[絵巻物]]。[[祭壇]]を構えて2匹の式神（右下）を従えた陰陽師・[[安倍晴明]]（右中央）が、姿を現した[[物の怪]]ども（左上）と対峙しつつ病身身代わりの[[祈り#神道|祈祷]]を行っている場面（物語の中盤）ここには描かれていないが、師・智興の[[臨終]]に際してその病難を我が身で肩代わりすることになる園城寺の[[僧]]・[[証空]]が物語の主人公である。その後、病難は[[不動明王]]が引き受け、証空は救済される。である。[[14世紀]]（[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝時代]]）の作。[[東京国立博物館]]所蔵{{Cite web|和書|title=不動利益縁起絵巻 |url=https://emuseum.nich.go.jp/detail?langId=ja&webView=&content_base_id=100284 |work=e国宝（公式ウェブサイト）|publisher=独立行政法人 [[国立文化財機構]][http://www.nich.go.jp/] |accessdate=2022-05-20}}。 | image2 = Nakifudo Engi Abe no Seimei.jpg | width2 = 270 | caption2 = 『泣不動縁起絵巻』（部分）
『不動利益縁起』と同じ内容である『泣不動縁起（なきふどうえんぎ）』の絵巻物。式神たちは右端に描かれている。[[室町時代]]の作。[[清浄華院]]所蔵{{Cite web|和書|title=泣不動縁起 |url=http://www6.ocn.ne.jp/~jozan/w_joke/nakihudou/nakihudou2.html |work=（公式ウェブサイト）|publisher=清浄華院 |accessdate=2011-12-16}}。 }} '''式神''' / '''識神'''（しきがみ、しきじん）は、[[陰陽師]]が使役する[[鬼|鬼神]]のことで、人心から起こる[[悪行]]や[[善|善行]]を見定める役を務めるもの。'''式の神''' / '''識の神'''（しきのかみ）ともいい、文献によっては、'''式鬼'''（しき）、'''式鬼神'''ともいう。 == 概要 == ｢式｣とは｢用いる｣の意味であり、使役することをあらわす。 === 鬼神 === # 荒ぶる神、または、妖怪変化のこと。 # [[霊魂|神霊]]のこと。 # [[仏教用語一覧|仏教用語]]としての、様々な不思議な現象を起こすとされる超人的存在のこと。 === 陰陽道 === {{独自研究|section=1|date=2012年8月}} [[陰陽道]]は[[神道]]（[[古神道]]）に[[道教]]の[[陰陽五行思想]]や、[[密教]]などの[[思想]]が執り入れられて[[習合]]したものであり、現在の[[神社神道]]にもその系譜としての思想や[[儀式]]が引き継がれている。[[神主]]や[[巫女]]は、[[憑依#神道・古神道|神降ろし]]によって[[神 (神道)|神]]を呼び出し[[憑依]]させることを「[[神楽]]」や「[[祈り#神道|祈祷]]」というが、これは[[荒魂・和魂|和御魂]]の神霊であり、式神は鬼神となっていることから、和御魂の神霊だけではなく、[[荒魂・和魂|荒御魂]]の神霊、いわゆる「荒ぶる神」や「妖怪変化」の類である位の低い神を呼び出し、使役したと考えられる。また、[[人間|人]]の[[善悪]]を監視するということは、人の[[心]]（[[霊魂]]）の和御魂と荒御魂の状態の変化でもあり、このことも式神という、2つの状態のどちらかの神霊を、使役したことと係わっていると思われる。 [[四国]]の[[高知県]]に伝わる陰陽道の一派である[[いざなぎ流]]では、'''式王子'''（しきおうじ）と呼ばれる。式王子という呼び名は明治頃から使われはじめ、それ以前はいざなぎ流でも職神と称していたことが文献から明らかになっている。 陰陽道の大家として知られる[[陰陽師]]・[[安倍晴明]]が使役したという'''式神'''に[[十二神将]]（[[十二天将]]）がある。 陰陽師もしくはそれを下地にした物語の式神は、平時には'''式札'''（しきふだ）と呼ばれる和紙札の状態にあるものが、陰陽師の術法によって使用されるとき、使役意図に適った能力を具える鳥獣や異形の者へと自在に[[変身]]する、そのような存在として描かれることが多い。[[12世紀]]末（[[平安時代]]末期- [[鎌倉時代]]初頭）頃を舞台とした[[安倍晴明]]（921- 1005年）が登場するには時期が合わないが、物語である。[[園城寺]]（三井寺）[[縁起]]である『不動利益縁起（ふどうりやくえんぎ）』に見られる式神は、[[室町時代]]から[[江戸時代]]にまでの、[[擬人化]]された[[ニワトリ|鶏]]や[[ウシ|牛]]の器物（[[道具]]）の妖怪と同じものであり、これは荒ぶる神としての式神をあらわしている。 == 丑の刻参り == [[ファイル:A woman makes a cursing ritual ceremony.jpg|thumb|right|280px|[[葛飾北斎]]の『[[北斎漫画]]』より、「[[丑の刻参り]]」の描き方を示した一図
神社の森にて丑の刻参りを行う女（中央）と、応えて姿を現した牛様の妖怪（中央）が描かれている。取り巻き（左右）は土着の[[天狗]]たち。[[1814年]]（[[文化 (元号)|文化]]11年）刊行。]] [[平安時代]]から続く「[[丑の刻参り]]」という[[呪い|呪詛]]は、[[神木]]（[[神体]]）に[[釘#五寸釘|五寸釘]]を打ち付け、自身が鬼となって恨む相手に[[復讐]]するというものである。[[丑]]の刻（午前1時から午前3時頃）に神木に釘を打って[[結界]]を破り、常夜（夜だけの神の国）から、禍をもたらす神（魔や妖怪）を呼び出し、[[憑依#神道・古神道|神懸り]]となって恨む相手を[[祟り|祟る]]と考えられていた。丑の刻参りもこのように、妖怪を呼び出し、祟るために使役する点においては同じといえる。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == {{Wiktionary pipe|en:式神|式神（英語版）}} * [[霊魂]] ** [[妖怪]] ** [[鬼]] ** [[使い魔]] * [[陰陽道]] * [[陰陽師]] {{デフォルトソート:しきかみ}} [[Category:陰陽道]] [[Category:呪術]] [[Category:日本神話]] [[Category:日本の神]] {{Japan-culture-stub}}

{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese [[:en:Folklore|Folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[:en:Inoue Nobutaka|Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[:en:Ghost|Ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] and ''[[:en:Emakimono|Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[:en:Folk religion|Folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[:en:Oni|Oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[:en:Megumi Fushiguro|Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>

It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] and ''[[:en:Emakimono|Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[:en:Folk religion|Folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[:en:Oni|Oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[:en:Megumi Fushiguro|Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>

Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[:en:Folk religion|Folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[:en:Oni|Oni]]-like nature.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شيكيغامي]][[as:শ্বিকিগামি]][[sn:commons:Category:Shikigami]][[de:Shikigami]][[en:Shikigami]][[es:Shikigami]][[fa:شیکیگامی]][[fr:Shikigami]][[id:Shikigami]][[it:Shikigami]][[ja:式神]][[ko:시키가미]][[mk:Шикигами]][[mni:ꯁꯤꯀꯤꯒꯃꯤ]][[no:Shikigami]][[pt:Shikigami]][[ru:Сикигами]][[tr:Shikigami]][[uk:Шікіґамі]][[vi:Shikigami]][[zh:式神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1573015]] Wikidata name: shikigami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Term for a being from Japanese folklore}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{distinct|Shinigami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=式神|date=March 2020}} [[File:Fudo-Rieki-Engi 01.jpg|right|thumb|240px|[[Abe no Seimei]] and his {{transl|ja|shikigami}} (bottom right) before an assembly of god-like demon spirits]]
{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese [[:en:Folklore|Folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[:en:Inoue Nobutaka|Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[:en:Ghost|Ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] and ''[[:en:Emakimono|Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[:en:Folk religion|Folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[:en:Oni|Oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[:en:Megumi Fushiguro|Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>

In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[:en:Folk religion|Folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[:en:Oni|Oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[:en:Megumi Fushiguro|Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شيكيغامي]][[as:শ্বিকিগামি]][[sn:commons:Category:Shikigami]][[de:Shikigami]][[en:Shikigami]][[es:Shikigami]][[fa:شیکیگامی]][[fr:Shikigami]][[id:Shikigami]][[it:Shikigami]][[ja:式神]][[ko:시키가미]][[mk:Шикигами]][[mni:ꯁꯤꯀꯤꯒꯃꯤ]][[no:Shikigami]][[pt:Shikigami]][[ru:Сикигами]][[tr:Shikigami]][[uk:Шікіґамі]][[vi:Shikigami]][[zh:式神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1573015]] Wikidata name: shikigami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Term for a being from Japanese folklore}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{distinct|Shinigami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=式神|date=March 2020}} [[File:Fudo-Rieki-Engi 01.jpg|right|thumb|240px|[[Abe no Seimei]] and his {{transl|ja|shikigami}} (bottom right) before an assembly of god-like demon spirits]]
{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese [[:en:Folklore|Folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[:en:Inoue Nobutaka|Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[:en:Ghost|Ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] and ''[[:en:Emakimono|Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[:en:Folk religion|Folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[:en:Oni|Oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[:en:Megumi Fushiguro|Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[:en:Dogū|Dogū]] * [[:en:Familiar|Familiar]] * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[:en:Kokeshi|Kokeshi]] * [[:en:Paper doll|Paper doll]] * [[:en:Pelesit|Pelesit]] * [[:en:Poppet|Poppet]] * [[:en:Spirit animal (disambiguation)|Spirit animal (disambiguation)]] * [[:en:Totem|Totem]] * [[:en:Ushabti|Ushabti]] * [[:en:Voodoo doll|Voodoo doll]] * [[:en:Zuijin|Zuijin]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شيكيغامي]][[as:শ্বিকিগামি]][[commons:Category:Shikigami]][[de:Shikigami]][[en:Shikigami]][[es:Shikigami]][[fa:شیکیگامی]][[fr:Shikigami]][[id:Shikigami]][[it:Shikigami]][[ja:式神]][[ko:시키가미]][[mk:Шикигами]][[mni:ꯁꯤꯀꯤꯒꯃꯤ]][[no:Shikigami]][[pt:Shikigami]][[ru:Сикигами]][[tr:Shikigami]][[uk:Шікіґамі]][[vi:Shikigami]][[zh:式神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شيكيغامي]][[as:শ্বিকিগামি]][[commons:Category:Shikigami]][[de:Shikigami]][[en:Shikigami]][[es:Shikigami]][[fa:شیکیگامی]][[fr:Shikigami]][[id:Shikigami]][[it:Shikigami]][[ja:式神]][[ko:시키가미]][[mk:Шикигами]][[mni:ꯁꯤꯀꯤꯒꯃꯤ]][[no:Shikigami]][[pt:Shikigami]][[ru:Сикигами]][[tr:Shikigami]][[uk:Шікіґамі]][[vi:Shikigami]][[zh:式神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شيكيغامي]][[as:শ্বিকিগামি]][[sn:commons:Category:Shikigami]][[de:Shikigami]][[en:Shikigami]][[es:Shikigami]][[fa:شیکیگامی]][[fr:Shikigami]][[id:Shikigami]][[it:Shikigami]][[ja:式神]][[ko:시키가미]][[mk:Шикигами]][[mni:ꯁꯤꯀꯤꯒꯃꯤ]][[no:Shikigami]][[pt:Shikigami]][[ru:Сикигами]][[tr:Shikigami]][[uk:Шікіґамі]][[vi:Shikigami]][[zh:式神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1573015]] Wikidata name: shikigami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Term for a being from Japanese folklore}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{distinct|Shinigami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=式神|date=March 2020}} [[File:Fudo-Rieki-Engi 01.jpg|right|thumb|240px|[[Abe no Seimei]] and his {{transl|ja|shikigami}} (bottom right) before an assembly of god-like demon spirits]]
{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese [[:en:Folklore|Folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[:en:Inoue Nobutaka|Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[:en:Ghost|Ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] and ''[[:en:Emakimono|Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[:en:Folk religion|Folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[:en:Oni|Oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[:en:Megumi Fushiguro|Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[:en:Dogū|Dogū]] * [[:en:Familiar|Familiar]] * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[:en:Kokeshi|Kokeshi]] * [[:en:Paper doll|Paper doll]] * [[:en:Pelesit|Pelesit]] * [[:en:Poppet|Poppet]] * [[:en:Spirit animal (disambiguation)|Spirit animal (disambiguation)]] * [[:en:Totem|Totem]] * [[:en:Ushabti|Ushabti]] * [[:en:Voodoo doll|Voodoo doll]] * [[:en:Zuijin|Zuijin]]
==References== {{reflist}}
{{Japanese folklore long}} ]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شيكيغامي]][[as:শ্বিকিগামি]][[commons:Category:Shikigami]][[de:Shikigami]][[en:Shikigami]][[es:Shikigami]][[fa:شیکیگامی]][[fr:Shikigami]][[id:Shikigami]][[it:Shikigami]][[ja:式神]][[ko:시키가미]][[mk:Шикигами]][[mni:ꯁꯤꯀꯤꯒꯃꯤ]][[no:Shikigami]][[pt:Shikigami]][[ru:Сикигами]][[tr:Shikigami]][[uk:Шікіґамі]][[vi:Shikigami]][[zh:式神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شيكيغامي]][[as:শ্বিকিগামি]][[commons:Category:Shikigami]][[de:Shikigami]][[en:Shikigami]][[es:Shikigami]][[fa:شیکیگامی]][[fr:Shikigami]][[id:Shikigami]][[it:Shikigami]][[ja:式神]][[ko:시키가미]][[mk:Шикигами]][[mni:ꯁꯤꯀꯤꯒꯃꯤ]][[no:Shikigami]][[pt:Shikigami]][[ru:Сикигами]][[tr:Shikigami]][[uk:Шікіґамі]][[vi:Shikigami]][[zh:式神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:شيكيغامي]][[as:শ্বিকিগামি]][[sn:commons:Category:Shikigami]][[de:Shikigami]][[en:Shikigami]][[es:Shikigami]][[fa:شیکیگامی]][[fr:Shikigami]][[id:Shikigami]][[it:Shikigami]][[ja:式神]][[ko:시키가미]][[mk:Шикигами]][[mni:ꯁꯤꯀꯤꯒꯃꯤ]][[no:Shikigami]][[pt:Shikigami]][[ru:Сикигами]][[tr:Shikigami]][[uk:Шікіґамі]][[vi:Shikigami]][[zh:式神]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1573015]] Wikidata name: shikigami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Term for a being from Japanese folklore}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{distinct|Shinigami}} {{Expand language|topic=|langcode=ja|otherarticle=式神|date=March 2020}} [[File:Fudo-Rieki-Engi 01.jpg|right|thumb|240px|[[Abe no Seimei]] and his {{transl|ja|shikigami}} (bottom right) before an assembly of god-like demon spirits]]
{{nihongo||式神|'''Shikigami'''}} (also read as {{nihongo||式の神|Shiki-no-kami}}) is the term for a being from [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese [[:en:Folklore|Folklore]]. According to the [[Shinto]] scholar [[:en:Inoue Nobutaka|Inoue Nobutaka]], it is thought to be some sort of {{transl|ja|[[kami]]}}, represented by a small [[:en:Ghost|Ghost]].<ref name="Inoue">{{cite book|last1=Inoue|first1=Nobutaka|title=An Encyclopedia of Shinto|date=2002|publisher=Kokugakuin University|location=Tokyo|isbn=978-4905853084|pages=84–90}}</ref> The belief of ''shikigami'' originates from ''[[Onmyōdō]]''. According to the tradition of ''Onmyōdō'', ''shikigami'' is a symbol of ''[[onmyōji]]''{{'}}s power because ''onmyōji'' can freely use ''shikigami'' with magical powers. It has been associated with "curses" since the 1000s of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], and was often depicted as a bird or a child in [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] and ''[[:en:Emakimono|Emakimono]]''.<ref name='hokkaido'>{{Cite web |url=http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|title=陰陽道の式神の成立と変遷 文学作品の呪詛にも触れつつ|pages=1|author=Wakako Nakajima|publisher=[[Hokkaido University of Education]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201218111057/http://s-ir.sap.hokkyodai.ac.jp/dspace/bitstream/123456789/9590/1/sap-kokugo-22-01.pdf|accessdate=6 February 2021|archive-date=2020-12-18}}</ref>
==Description== ''Shikigami'' are conjured beings, made alive through a complex conjuring ceremony. Their power is connected to the spiritual force of their master, where if the invoker is well introduced and has much experience, their ''shiki'' can possess animals and even people and manipulate them, but if the invoker is careless, their ''shikigami'' may get out of control in time, gaining its own will and consciousness and can even raid its own master and kill them in revenge. Usually ''shikigami'' are conjured to exercise risky orders for their masters, such as spying, stealing and enemy tracking. ''Shikigami'' are said to be invisible most of the time, but they can be made visible by binding them into small, folded and artfully cut paper manikins. There are also ''shikigami'' that can show themselves as animals.<ref name="Inoue"/><ref>{{cite book|last1=Avant|first1=G. Rodney|title=A Mythological Reference|date=2005|publisher=AuthorHouse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1418492786|page=356}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Drazen|first1=Patrick|title=A Gathering of Spirits: Japan's Ghost Story Tradition: from Folklore and Kabuki to Anime and Manga|date=2011|publisher=Iuniverse|location=Bloomington, Indiana|isbn=978-1462029426|page=224}}</ref>
In the ''Izanagi-ryū'' ({{langx|ja|いざなぎ流}}) [[:en:Folk religion|Folk religion]], the most elite ''onmyōji'' could also conjure an exceptionally powerful type of ''shikigami'' called a ''shikiōji'' ({{langx|ja|式王子}}) to ward off disasters or demons that cause sickness. Regular mystics could not attempt to summon it without risking losing control of it due to its [[:en:Oni|Oni]]-like nature.
==In popular culture== Within the realm of the manga and anime series ''[[:en:Jujutsu Kaisen|Jujutsu Kaisen]]'', shikigami play a pivotal role in the arsenal of jujutsu sorcerers, notably [[:en:Megumi Fushiguro|Megumi Fushiguro]]. The supernatural entities are conjured through the manipulation of cursed energy, typically facilitated by talismans and summoning magic.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Yehia |first=Ahmed |date=2024-02-06 |title=Jujutsu Kaisen: What are Shikigami? |url=https://gamerant.com/jujutsu-kaisen-what-are-shikigami/ |access-date=2024-03-05 |website=Game Rant |language=en}}</ref>
== See also == * [[:en:Dogū|Dogū]] * [[:en:Familiar|Familiar]] * [[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]] * [[Hōko (doll)]] * [[:en:Kokeshi|Kokeshi]] * [[:en:Paper doll|Paper doll]] * [[:en:Pelesit|Pelesit]] * [[:en:Poppet|Poppet]] * [[:en:Spirit animal (disambiguation)|Spirit animal (disambiguation)]] * [[:en:Totem|Totem]] * [[:en:Ushabti|Ushabti]] * [[:en:Voodoo doll|Voodoo doll]] * [[:en:Zuijin|Zuijin]]
==References== {{reflist}}
{{Japanese folklore long}} ]]
